EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front over the Great Lakes moved through Southern New England during the late afternoon and early night of May 15th.  The air mass over Rhode Island was very warm and humid, and as the cold front moved into this air it generated numerous thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms moving east from Connecticut produced damaging wind gusts in Western Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 5:13 PM EST, a large tree was blocking North Road in Hopkinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the Lower Mississippi Valley moved northeast and passed offshore of Nantucket, bringing snow to much of Southern New England from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From five to six inches of snow fell on Northwest Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm passed offshore of Nantucket on January 30, while a surge of colder air moved in from the Great Lakes.  This brought several inches of snow to Eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island during the night of the 29th and the day of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From five to eight inches of snow fell on Newport County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over New York City early in the morning on November 3rd rapidly intensified as it moved northeastward across New England. Heavy rain occurred in the early morning hours, with generally 1.50 to 2.50 inches in eastern sections of southern New England and up to 3.66 inches in the slopes of the Berkshires. A few severe thunderstorms moved from Rhode Island into eastern Massachusetts around daybreak. As the strong low passed to our north, strong to damaging westerly winds developed during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 149 PM EDT, a mesonet site at Block Island Jetty recorded a wind gust to 58 mph and at 209 PM EDT, the same site measured sustained winds of 47 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low  pressure moving out of the Ohio Valley passed south of Southern New England on the 2nd and moved out to sea on the 3rd.  This storm brought heavy rain and strong winds to much of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2:53 PM EST an amateur radio operator at Charlestown reported a wind gust to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low  pressure moving out of the Ohio Valley passed south of Southern New England on the 2nd and moved out to sea on the 3rd.  This storm brought heavy rain and strong winds to much of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2:43 PM EST a mesonet sensor on the north side of Block Island reported a wind gust to 71 mph. At 11:06 AM EST the Co-operative observer at Block Island reported a wind gust to 59 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3:58 PM EST the Automated Surface Observation System platform at T. F. Green Airport measured a wind gust to 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved up from the Carolinas, bringing snow and rain to Southern New England on March 7th and 8th.  This was primarily a snow event in Northwest Rhode Island, but with 1 to 3 inches of rain falling on Central and Southern Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From six to thirteen inches of snow fell on Northwest Providence County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: From four to six inches of snow fell on Western Kent County. At 8:58 PM EST a tree was down on wires on Town Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure along the Carolina coast March 12 moved up the coast and passed offshore of Southern New England on March 13, moving off through the Maritimes on March 14. The storm brought snow accumulations of up to two feet in Northern Rhode Island and up to one foot in Southern Rhode Island. The storm also brought wind gusts of up to fifty miles per hour to coastal Rhode Island.  Blizzard conditions were observed at Newport State Airport in Middletown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From twelve to seventeen inches of snow fell on Southeast Providence County.

At 8:00 PM EST a trained spotter in Portsmouth reported a tree and wires down on Corys Lane.

At 10 AM EST a tree and wires were down on Queen Awashunk's Trail in Little Compton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early-season nor'easter moved from the Mid-Atlantic coast to southeastern Massachusetts on the 15th and away from the region on the 16th. A quick thump of moderate to heavy snow occurred in Rhode Island on the front end of the storm, with most accumulations over with by midnight on the 16th. Snowfall amounts ranged from just a coasting along the immediate south coast to 9 inches in the higher hills in northwest Rhode Island. Strong east to northeast winds battered coastal areas ahead of the low pressure system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on numerous reports from CoCoRaHS, spotters, the public, media, and an NWS employee, snowfall averaged 5.5 inches in southeast Providence County. The measurements ranged from 4.6 inches to 6.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure organized over the Ohio Valley on April 14th, swept to the Mid Atlantic coast on the 15th, and moved along the New England coast on the 16th.  This brought rain and some heavy downpours to the coastal plain on the 16th, while inland areas had a mixture of snow, freezing rain, and rain.  Strong gusty southeast winds also affected Rhode Island on the 16th. The storm moved off through the Canadian Maritimes the night of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3:52 PM EST a tree was reported down on Howard Hill Road in Foster.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1:50 PM EST, a tree was reported down on Reservoir Road near State Route 10 in Cranston.  At 3:57 PM EST, the Automated Surface Observing System near door in Warwick at T. F. Green Airport measured a wind gust to 47 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved north from the Florida coast and deepened, passing southeast of Nantucket on Thursday January 4.  The storm reached its lowest pressure and strongest pressure gradient as it passed Southern New England. This brought heavy snow and damaging winds to Rhode Island, and caused blizzard conditions at Block Island and near-blizzard conditions at other locations in the state. The storm moved off through the Maritimes on January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve to seventeen inches of snow fell on Bristol County Rhode Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front crossed New England from the afternoon of January 12 to the morning of January 13. Strong southerly winds ahead of the front drew mild and humid air north over Southern New England. This helped generate heavy downpours before the front moved through.  Rainfall amounts ranged from one to three inches, except in Southeast Massachusetts where amounts ranged up to four inches.  Maximum wind gusts ranged from 45 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 859 AM EST, Fletcher Avenue in Cranston was reported closed.  Standing water to at least one foot depth filled the road across several blocks.  Rainfall between three and four inches was measured in the vicinity of Cranston.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: From five to seven inches fell on Northwest Providence County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10:25 PM EST, cars were reported partly submerged in an apartment complex on Oaklawn Avenue in Cranston; flooding also took place on the property in front of the complex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into very warm humid air over Rhode Island on September 6th. This brought showers and strong thunderstorms to the region during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3:49 PM EST, wind brought a large tree down in Cumberland on Diamond Hill Road at Tower Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric trough moved over Rhode Island on the 4th, tapping high amounts of moisture in the air to cause heavy showers that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10:36 AM EST, a car was trapped in flooding at an underpass on Wilbur Avenue near the intersection with Oaklawn Avenue in Cranston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 28, a storm passing west of Rhode Island strong winds to the South Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3:36 PM EST, a tree was reported down on West Main Road, near the Melville Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Departing coastal low pressure brought strong winds and scattered snow showers to Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 8:02 AM EST a large tree was reported down on Wood Street in Providence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England late on July 28th.  This created thunderstorms that moved across Southern Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 9:10 PM EST, lightning struck a house on Wolf Rock Road in Exeter. A large tree and wires were also brought down.  No smoke reported in house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England on July 6th. This caused numerous thunderstorms, some with locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 11:20 AM EST, a trained spotter reported flooding from heavy rain on Industrial Road in Cumberland.  People on the road were unable to exit their vehicles.  Rainfall of 1.25 inches was reported in Cumberland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across southern New England early in the morning on the 28th.  Gusty southwest winds prevailed on Oct. 28th in the morning, behind the storm.  After having had storm and gale conditions on the 27th, high surf still lingered on the coastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf, on the order of 6 to 8 foot waves, knocked a 51 year old woman off the rocks, where she was fishing.  She ended up drowning.  Her 52 year old husband went into the water to save her but he ended up drowning as well.  Very sad story.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east from the Great Lakes on Monday October 15th. Strong southwest winds ahead of the front moved across Rhode Island during the late evening and night of the 15th, then became strong northwest after the cold front moved through. Winds then diminished on the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph were reported. There were several reports of trees and wires down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 11:55 PM EST, a tree was reported down on the Putnam Pike in Glocester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the Gulf of Mexico moved up the East Coast on October 27th and 28th, bringing rain and strong winds to mainly the eastern half of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Richmond, at 1010 AM EDT, a tree was down on wires on Ranch Road.  In Westerly, at 1246 PM EDT, a wind gust to 59 mph was reported by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the Gulf of Mexico moved up the East Coast on October 27th and 28th, bringing rain and strong winds to mainly the eastern half of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1240 PM EDT in Providence, a tree was down on wires on Sunbury Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the Gulf of Mexico moved up the East Coast on October 27th and 28th, bringing rain and strong winds to mainly the eastern half of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 914 AM EDT, sustained winds of 40 mph were recorded at the Block Island Jetty mesonet and at 1104 AM EDT, sustained winds of 43 mph were recorded there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over upstate New York moved east-southeastward across northern Rhode Island and southeast Massachusetts during the afternoon and early evening.  A warm frontal boundary extending eastward from the low, combined with strong mid-level wind shear, provided the focus for tornado development, both in RI and MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially touched down in North Providence on Meadow View Blvd. around 331 PM EDT, where large branches were downed, including one on a car and a fence.  In this area, wind speeds were estimated to be 60-70 mph, which is classified as EF-0 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.  From there, damage was discontinuous as the tornado headed northeastward toward the southwest part of Lincoln, with only a few treetops snapped and downed branches.  In Lincoln, the tornado caused extensive damage on Angell Road and Riata Drive around 333 PM EDT.  There were numerous uprooted or snapped trees, shingles blown off several houses, and large branches downed.  Wind speeds were estimated to be 90-100 mph, which is classified as EF-1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.  Damage became more sporadic farther north on Elden Court, Dennell Drive, and Christopher Drive, where a few large trees and branches were downed.  Winds in those areas were estimated at 60-80 mph, which is classified as EF-0.  The tornado lifted near Christopher Drive around 334 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over New York City early in the morning on November 3rd rapidly intensified as it moved northeastward across New England. Heavy rain occurred in the early morning hours, with generally 1.50 to 2.50 inches in eastern sections of southern New England and up to 3.66 inches in the slopes of the Berkshires. A few severe thunderstorms moved from Rhode Island into eastern Massachusetts around daybreak. As the strong low passed to our north, strong to damaging westerly winds developed during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Glocester at 207 PM EDT, a tree was down on Reservoir Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early-season nor'easter moved from the Mid-Atlantic coast to southeastern Massachusetts on the 15th and away from the region on the 16th. A quick thump of moderate to heavy snow occurred in Rhode Island on the front end of the storm, with most accumulations over with by midnight on the 16th. Snowfall amounts ranged from just a coasting along the immediate south coast to 9 inches in the higher hills in northwest Rhode Island. Strong east to northeast winds battered coastal areas ahead of the low pressure system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 155 AM, a mesonet recorded a peak gust to 59 mph at Point Judith and at 200 AM it recorded a 42 mph sustained wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on numerous reports from CoCoRaHS, spotters, the public, and the media, snowfall averaged 7.5 inches in northwest Providence County. The measurements ranged from 5.0 inches in Woonsocket to 9.0 inches in Burrillville.
